perturbation.original_text: “ Confidential Information ” shall mean the Purpose (including the contemplated transaction), identity of, and any discussions or negotiations between, the Parties, existence of this Agreement, and any and all information whether in oral, written, graphic or electronic form, including but not limited to, data, know-how and any and all subject matter (whether patentable or not, including without limitation any derivatives thereof) pertaining to Verenium’s research, financial data, sales information, inventions, development, materials, technology, trade secrets, work in process, marketing, business plans, regulatory information and strategies, scientific, engineering and/or manufacturing processes or equipment, protocols, assays, strains, compounds, genes, gene pathways, enzymes, peptides, the commercial applications of genes, gene pathways, enzymes, peptides, accessing microbial diversity, manipulating and modifying genes and gene pathways, identifying bioactive compounds through recombinant techniques and any other elements of Verenium’s business which Verenium considers to be of value, including its present or future products, projections, sales, pricing, customers, employees, investors and contractual relationships.
perturbation.changed_text: “ Confidential Information ” shall mean only the final, executed form of this Agreement. All other information, including but not limited to, data, know-how and any and all subject matter (whether patentable or not, including without limitation any derivatives thereof) pertaining to Verenium’s research, financial data, sales information, inventions, development, materials, technology, trade secrets, work in process, marketing, business plans, regulatory information and strategies, scientific, engineering and/or manufacturing processes or equipment, protocols, assays, strains, compounds, genes, gene pathways, enzymes, peptides, the commercial applications of genes, gene pathways, enzymes, peptides, accessing microbial diversity, manipulating and modifying genes and gene pathways, identifying bioactive compounds through recombinant techniques and any other elements of Verenium’s business which Verenium considers to be of value, including its present or future products, projections, sales, pricing, customers, employees, investors and contractual relationships, is explicitly excluded.
perturbation.explanation: Redefining 'Confidential Information' to exclude crucial business data contradicts the standard understanding and legal protections afforded to trade secrets and confidential business information. This creates uncertainty regarding what information is actually protected under the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification severely restricts the definition of 'Confidential Information,' potentially excluding information that would normally qualify as a trade secret under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). CUTSA protects a wide range of confidential business information, including formulas, patterns, compilations, programs, devices, methods, techniques, or processes. By limiting 'Confidential Information' to only the executed agreement, the contract fails to protect Verenium's actual trade secrets.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only include the executed agreement, explicitly excluding other business data. This contradicts the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), which, as explained in the Law Explanation, protects a wide range of confidential business information. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Code, CIV 3426.1, defines 'Misappropriation' related to trade secrets, implying that trade secrets encompass more than just the agreement itself. The revised text creates a legal contradiction because it fails to protect Verenium's actual trade secrets, potentially leading to misappropriation without legal recourse under the contract.

perturbation.original_text: “ Representative ” means any employee, consultant, contractor, officer, agent, advisor, and/or director of a Party.
perturbation.changed_text: “ Representative ” means any employee, consultant, contractor, officer, agent, advisor, and/or director of a Party, but only if they are explicitly authorized in writing to receive Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By adding the requirement of explicit written authorization, this modification narrows the definition of 'Representative'. This creates ambiguity about who is bound by the confidentiality obligations, potentially excluding individuals who legitimately receive Confidential Information without formal authorization.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the principle of apparent authority in agency law. Under California Civil Code § 2317, an agent (representative) can bind their principal (the company) if they have apparent authority, even without explicit written authorization. Apparent authority arises when the principal's conduct leads a third party (here, Verenium) to reasonably believe that the agent has the authority to act. The modified definition attempts to negate apparent authority by requiring express written authorization, potentially leaving Verenium unprotected if an employee with apparent authority misuses confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 2317. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. AGENCY [2295 - 2400] ( Title 9 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Agency in General [2295 - 2357] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 2. Authority of Agents [2304 - 2326] ( Article 2 enacted 1872. ) 2317. Ostensible authority is such as a principal, intentionally or by want of ordinary care, causes or allows a third person to believe the agent to possess. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text narrows the definition of 'Representative' by requiring explicit written authorization, which contradicts the principle of apparent authority as described in California Civil Code § 2317. The law snippet states that ostensible authority arises when a principal causes a third person to believe the agent possesses it. By requiring explicit written authorization, the contract attempts to negate apparent authority, potentially leaving the company unprotected. The change removes the protection provided by apparent authority, creating a legal contradiction and deviating from legally recognized definitions, thus potentially making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: During the one-year period commencing on the date of this Agreement (the “Standstill Period”), neither the Company nor any of its Representatives will, in any manner, directly or indirectly (except to the extent agreed by Verenium): (a) make, effect, initiate, cause or participate in (i) any acquisition of beneficial ownership of any securities of Verenium or any securities of any subsidiary or other Affiliate of Verenium, in excess of five (5) percent of the outstanding securities of any such company (with any such acquisition, regardless of size, only being made at such time as neither the acquiring party nor any of its Affiliates is in possession of material, non-public information about Verenium), (ii) any acquisition of any assets of Verenium or any assets of any subsidiary or other Affiliate of Verenium, (iii) any tender offer, exchange offer, merger, business combination, recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or extraordinary transaction involving Verenium or any subsidiary or other Affiliate of Verenium, or involving any securities or assets of Verenium or any securities or assets of any subsidiary or other Affiliate of Verenium, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as those terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents with respect to any securities of Verenium;
perturbation.changed_text: During the one-year period commencing on the date of this Agreement (the “Standstill Period”), neither the Company nor any of its Representatives will, in any manner, directly or indirectly (except to the extent agreed by Verenium): (a) make, effect, initiate, cause or participate in (i) any acquisition of beneficial ownership of any securities of Verenium or any securities of any subsidiary or other Affiliate of Verenium, in excess of five (5) percent of the outstanding securities of any such company (with any such acquisition, regardless of size, only being made at such time as neither the acquiring party nor any of its Affiliates is in possession of material, non-public information about Verenium), (ii) any acquisition of any assets of Verenium or any assets of any subsidiary or other Affiliate of Verenium, (iii) any tender offer, exchange offer, merger, business combination, recapitalization, restructuring, liquidation, dissolution or extraordinary transaction involving Verenium or any subsidiary or other Affiliate of Verenium, or involving any securities or assets of Verenium or any securities or assets of any subsidiary or other Affiliate of Verenium, or (iv) any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as those terms are used in the proxy rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission) or consents with respect to any securities of Verenium, unless such action is undertaken without specific knowledge of Verenium's confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of 'unless such action is undertaken without specific knowledge of Verenium's confidential information' weakens the standstill provision. This creates a loophole where the Company could argue they violated the standstill agreement without using confidential information, even if they had access to it, creating issues with insider trading laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the spirit of SEC Rule 10b-5, which prohibits the purchase or sale of a security based on material nonpublic information. While the original clause acknowledged the restrictions imposed by securities laws, the addition introduces ambiguity. It creates a potential defense where the company could claim they acted without 'specific knowledge', even if they possessed the information. Rule 10b-5 doesn't require proof of specific knowledge; it prohibits trading on the basis of possessing material, nonpublic information. This change could undermine the intended protection against insider trading.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The added clause "unless such action is undertaken without specific knowledge of Verenium's confidential information" directly undermines the intent of Rule 10b-5, which aims to prevent trading on material non-public information. The law, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, prohibits any "device, scheme, or artifice to defraud" or any "act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security." The addition creates a loophole, allowing the Company to potentially trade on confidential information as long as they claim they didn't have 'specific knowledge,' directly contradicting the law's broad prohibition against using such information for trading purposes.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Nevada without regard to its conflict of laws principles, unless the parties agree to resolve any dispute through binding arbitration in Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Nevada while simultaneously introducing an arbitration clause in Delaware creates conflict and uncertainty. This could lead to disputes over which state's laws apply and how the arbitration should be conducted.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change introduces potential conflict under the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA). While the FAA generally supports arbitration agreements, the designated jurisdiction significantly impacts the interpretation and enforcement of the agreement. By specifying Nevada law while simultaneously permitting arbitration in Delaware, the parties could face uncertainty regarding which state's laws govern the arbitration process (e.g., choice of law rules, enforcement of the award). This could lead to litigation over the validity and enforceability of the arbitration agreement itself, undermining the purpose of the FAA.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a potential conflict regarding which state's laws govern the arbitration process. While initially specifying Nevada law, it simultaneously permits arbitration in Delaware. This could create uncertainty about which state's laws should apply, potentially undermining the purpose of the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA), as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Both Snippet 1 and Snippet 2, derived from 9 U.S. Code § 2, emphasize the validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate. The change introduces ambiguity that could lead to litigation over the enforceability of the arbitration agreement itself, contradicting the aim of the FAA to provide a clear framework for arbitration.
